data.title: Enagas: I Don't Like The Hydrogen Focus
data.description:
Introduction

I used to like Enagas (OTCPK:ENGGF) (OTCPK:ENGGY) as the Spanish natural gas transmission company and LNG terminal operator owned a critical piece of infrastructure in the Spanish energy landscape. Unfortunately, the company has been plagued by reduced regulated revenues, and I’m not a big fan of its hydrogen push either. The share price has performer pretty poorly in the past few years, but the total return is still approximately zero thanks to the strong dividends. As the company has now published its 2025-2030 Strategic Outlook, this could be a good moment to have another look at this Spanish company.

Enagas has its primary listing in Madrid, where it’s trading with ENG as its ticker symbol. The average daily volume in Spain is approximately 1.2M shares, so it is for sure the most liquid listing. As Enagas also reports its financial results in Euro, I will use the EUR as the base currency throughout this article.

2024 again wasn’t an easy year

In 2024, Enagas reported a total revenue of approximately 913M EUR, which represents a decrease of just under 1% compared to the almost 920M EUR it recorded in 2023. The company also had to deal with a lower contribution from affiliates (as its stake in Tallgrass Energy was sold during the year) but this was somewhat compensated by the lower interest expenses.

As the image above shows, the bottom-line result showed a 3.2% increase to 310M EUR before taking the impact of the asset sales into account. Those had a non-recurring net impact of a negative 609M EUR, which means the bottom line showed a net loss of 299M EUR.

In Enagas’ case, it makes sense to have a look at the FFO result considering Enagas runs an asset-heavy business, but its sustaining capex tends to be substantially lower than the depreciation expenses. Fortunately, Enagas provides a nice detailed breakdown of its cash flows and as you can see below, its FFO came in at 689M EUR thanks to a lower tax and lower interest payments.

The free cash flow result was 1.39B EUR, but this, of course, including the 1B EUR cash inflow related to the sale of its stake in Tallgrass Energy.

That being said, Enagas dramatically increased its liquidity as the company sold its stake in Tallgrass Energy for $1.1B and right before Christmas, it was awarded a $194M verdict as the ICSID sided with Enagas and condemned Peru for violating the Spain-Peru investment protection agreement.

The $194M is just the beginning, as Enagas highlighted two additional elements that have not been taken into consideration yet, but I’m not taking those potential cash windfalls into consideration just yet.

The sale of the Tallgrass stake allowed Enagas to reduce its net debt from 3.35B EUR to 2.4B EUR at the end of 2024 (this does not take the Peruvian $194M award into consideration) and this resulted in an increase in the credit rating and should result in a 40 bps decrease in the average cost of debt in 2025 and 2026.

Enagas also published its guidance for 2025. The company anticipates an EBITDA result of 670M EUR, which should result in a net profit of 265M EUR. The dividend projection remained unchanged, and Enagas expects to once again pay a 1.00 EUR cash dividend (subject to the standard 19% dividend withholding tax in Spain).

The 2030 strategic plan

Although the world seems to be backing down from green energy and hydrogen, Enagas is still planning to execute on its Hydrogen strategy. Between 2025 and 2030, the company plans to invest 4.04B EUR, of which almost 80% will be earmarked for hydrogen development. Interestingly, Enagas estimates these investments will allow it to increase its EBITDA by 9.5% per year between 2026 and 2030. But as the company divested some assets, the EBITDA will decrease in 2025, which means the EBITDA growth based on the 2024 result will be just 2.5% per year.

Considering the EBITDA was 761M EUR in 2024, applying an annual growth rate of 2.5% until 2030 implies a FY 2030 EBITDA result of 882M EUR. This also represents a 32% increase from the FY 2025 EBITDA guidance.

While that sounds attractive, let’s not forget the company will likely see its net debt increase from 2.4B EUR at the end of 2024 to likely 5-5.5B EUR by the end of 2030 after completing the 4B EUR investment program.

Although it is nice to see Enagas is looking into diversifying its asset profile, I’m just not convinced running up the net debt by 4B EUR to generate 210M EUR in additional EBITDA just doesn’t feel like the best use of capital.

Investment thesis

Whereas other companies in Europe are scaling back their commitments to green energy, Enagas is still taking the plunge. It may be a great investment if everything works out and hydrogen does indeed enjoy a strong take-up rate, but given the disappointing EBITDA growth projections between now and 2030, I’m not overly excited about the plans to thoroughly expand the hydrogen network.

The company has pledged to keep its dividend unchanged at 1 EUR for both FY 2025 and FY 2026, but as this results in a pretty strong cash outflow of in excess of a quarter of a billion euro per year, I fear the dividend may come under pressure, and it will be interesting to see Enagas’ commitment to dividends beyond FY 2026.

I currently have a small position in Enagas as I like the natural gas transmission business, but the combination of the focus on hydrogen and the sale of Tallgrass in the USA wouldn’t be my preferred way forward. Enagas has been a good dividend payer and that’s why my total return is still (slightly) positive despite sitting on an unrealized capital loss, but I’m not buying any more shares at this point. I’m a ‘hold’ at this point in time.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Seabridge Gold Has Incredible Value As Momentum Builds On Record Gold Rally (Rating Upgrade)
data.description:
A “Buy” Rating for the shares of Seabridge Gold

With this analysis, we believe it would not be unreasonable to raise the recommendation rating to a "Buy" from the previous article's rating of "Hold" for NYSE-listed shares of Seabridge Gold Inc. (SA).

Hereinafter referred to simply as "Seabridge" or "SA", this stock represents a Canadian developer of gold and copper properties in Canada and the US state of Nevada.

About Seabridge and its North American properties.

In the previous article, we wrote:

“ Seabridge owns 100% of the interests in several North American gold projects.

These are:

• The Kerr-Sulphurets-Mitchell Project (hereinafter "KSM") and the Iskut Project. Both are located in northwestern British Columbia, Canada's "Golden Triangle".

• The Courageous Lake Project in Canada's Northwest Territories.

• The Snowstorm Project in the Getchell Gold Belt in northern Nevada.

• The 3 Aces Project in the Yukon Territory. ”

Although Seabridge does not currently sell gold but plans to do so in the future as the company owns gold properties that it plans to convert into gold mines, its shares are still positively correlated with the price of gold mainly.

Stock Performance Since Previous Hold Rating

While the previous article acknowledged Seabridge's tremendous value, which is gaining momentum with rising gold and copper prices, it also pointed to the risk of strong headwinds. It was noted that interest rates could remain elevated for longer than generally expected due to inflation concerns, while stock prices rose sharply compared to previous trends and some indicators and the subsequent downward trend could be on the verge of being quite disruptive. In early November, rumors began to swirl that President Donald Trump was planning a series of drastic measures, including significant tariff increases on certain US imports, a crackdown on illegal immigration, drastic tax cuts, and deregulation. Although these measures were intended to stimulate the American economy, they contributed to a climate of great uncertainty, particularly regarding inflation. Markets feared that the disinflation process could stall or even lead to renewed sharp price increases. The so-called “Trump trade.” This new trend in the market narrative met with little enthusiasm among those hoping for lower interest rates from the central bank. For a company like Seabridge Gold, the prospect of a continued higher-for-longer posture on loans means that the construction of the KSM-Iron Cap-Snowfield project will have to be further postponed. Although ArcWest Exploration Inc. (AWX:CA) calls it a “super-giant” porphyry copper-gold deposit, we believe the project cannot enter the most exciting phase of its development without the provision of equally massive capital. We had already issued a similar warning with a “Hold” rating in our previous analysis, smoothing the more optimistic market sentiment that was circulating in the market at the time. Having offered psychological preparation to take the hit with our previous opinion on this stock, it is therefore not entirely unexpected that Seabridge Gold Inc. stock price showed a negative change of 29.81%, which was worse than the S&P 500, which also experienced a negative change of 5.62% at the time of writing.

Yet, “Every misfortune has its upside,” we add. We signaled to wait for the right moment to take advantage of the sharply lower share price. Now, given the tremendous value recognized in Seabridge Gold Inc.’s portfolio, which is poised to gain momentum as the macroeconomic mix turns in its favor, it greatly supports our Buy rating. But always moderately, by taking advantage of stock market price lows, as guided by 14-day relative strength index patterns, volatility is a good friend here in providing an effective tool to reduce risk.

We Get Impulses For The Increase In The Rating Thanks To The Following Points

First: Given the recent renewed wave of high optimism about the price of gold, it is easy to imagine that the profitability of Seabridge's future gold production could be affected. By influencing market sentiment, this could be reflected in the market value of the shares on the NYSE, in our opinion.

The market price of gold, measured by Gold Spot Price (XAUUS$:CUR), was (at the time of writing) at $3,121.82/ounce, up 18.95% year-to-date and 36.62% year-over-year. This is due to strong demand for safe haven assets amid geopolitical risks and uncertainty surrounding President Donald Trump's plans to revive the American dream, including increased tariffs. These factors prompted UBS and Macquarie to raise their price forecasts earlier this month to $3,200/ounce and $3,500/ounce, respectively. Last week, Goldman Sachs and Bank of America raised their gold price forecasts to US$ 3,300/ounce (from US$ 3,100/ounce) by 2025 and US$ 3,500/ounce within the next two years, respectively.

Seabridge’s projects also focus on copper production, so we want to update you on the metal’s price development and analysts’ forecasts for the coming period. Copper futures (HG1:COM) were trading at $5.03 per pound at the time of writing. Copper prices have risen by 26.25% so far this year and are up 22.45% year-over-year at the time of writing. Prices fell slightly this week as to lock in profits traders took advantage of last week’s record highs on an unprecedented surge in copper imports into the United States to avoid higher tariffs announced by US President Donald Trump on all red metal entering the country. Copper is a key metal for building electric cars and solar panels and powering AI data centers around the world. As such, demand is expected to increase in the future, but supply will always be tight due to ongoing geopolitical, macroeconomic, and geophysical factors affecting the availability of existing mineral resources.

Looking ahead, according to global macroeconomic models and analysts at Trading Economics (see the “ Forecast” section of the website), copper prices are projected to trade at US$5.50 in 12 months.

Second: The Seabridge portfolio appears to have been strengthened by a productive year in 2024, which, we believe, was as such last year due to the fundamental progress in the Iskut project and the Kerr-Sulphurets-Mitchell project, both located in northwestern British Columbia, Canada's “Golden Triangle”. We have described these improvements in detail in our previous Seabridge reviews and will only summarize them briefly here as follows: At Iskut, exploration activities are contributing to increasing awareness of the robust porphyry copper-gold potential that is slowly coming to light. The Kerr-Sulphurets-Mitchell Project has received two fundamental licenses from local regulators, including the important province of British Columbia, to unlock the growth potential of this crown jewel in Seabridge's portfolio: A) A green light extending a license to exploit a large mineral property containing over 11 mineral claims for an additional 20 years. B) The “Substantially Started Designation,” lifting all time limits on environmental approvals. By removing the various temporal and spatial restrictions and allowing freedom of action on the property, these permits contribute significantly to de-risking the portfolio of Seabridge. In this article, the analysis is based on new elements that have emerged since our last coverage of Seabridge stock.

Third: Last month, the company successfully raised US$100 MM through the issuance of new shares, and the company will use the capital to move forward with its exploration and development activities at KSM.

Rudi Fronk, Chairman and CEO of Seabridge, said:

"the financing ensures continued forward momentum at KSM while we engage with our short list of prospective joint-venture partners. This year's program will provide the remaining field data that we anticipate will be required for the bankable feasibility study which we expect to undertake with our joint-venture partner.

Regarding KSM's 2022 Preliminary Feasibility Study ("2022 PFS") in our last article on Seabridge, we wrote:

“ The 2022 PFS indicates that, […], KSM is expected to produce an average of 1.03 million ounces of gold, 178 million pounds of copper and 3 million ounces of silver annually over the initial 33-year mine life. The ounces are mined from the Mitchell, East Mitchell and Sulphurets open pits (excluding Courageous Lake) and include 47.3 million ounces of gold, 160 million ounces of silver and 7,320 million pounds of copper in proven and probable reserves.”

With extensive, multi-year/low-cost production of gold and key metals for electrification and electric vehicles, AI data centers, and solar panels, KSM has the potential to create long-term income and contribute to the social well-being of entire communities in British Columbia. The price environment for gold, especially, and also copper, provides an even more favorable backdrop for the hope of a highly profitable metal production project at KSM.

Major players in the global mining industry cannot ignore the value of the KSM project, which could be integrated into their growth plans. These major players may already be vying for a place in a future joint venture with Seabridge. These renowned names in the global mining industry would undoubtedly also have the necessary liquidity to develop the KSM project into a metal-producing mine in a shorter timeframe.

Yet, despite the enormous liquidity at their disposal, these well-known gold giants do not want to obscure the role traditionally reserved for the financial system. Rather, they want lenders to participate in particularly large and ambitious projects like KSM, because, figuratively speaking, it is a “quid pro quo” – everyone benefits as best they can, and capital-intensive industries like mining have no choice but to adapt to this development in the relationship between banks and businesses.

While KSM is a project with great value potential, a vision that is currently being stimulated by metal prices that are trying to outdo themselves by breaking all records, we believe that the high borrowing costs are still a reason why the big fish has not yet come to the open and revealed itself in partnership with Seabridge on the KSM project if the financial system is to be involved.

The tariff war is the new obstacle that stands between the Seabridge KSM project and significant financing, which will be delayed as long as the “big fish” does not resolve the hesitation and take the vacant spot in the joint venture. If it were up to the KSM project itself and its growth prospects, as there is no question of their great appeal, the problem would probably have been solved by now. The movement in the share price of Seabridge Gold, which suffered a resounding setback in November, around the time when President Donald Trump's tariffs were on everyone's mind, provides very credible evidence for this thesis. Given the increasing uncertainty emanating from the US's international trading partners, who are now also threatening to increase tariffs on their imports of American products in retaliation for Trump's tariffs, there are fears that inflation could flare up again. Therefore, Western central banks, led by the Federal Reserve, are forced to stop, reduce, postpone, or even raise the next interest rate cut. This is something that the President of the Federal Reserve Bank of St. Louis, Alberto Musalem, for example, has not ruled out, believing that President Trump's tariff policy could push inflation up again. As borrowing costs decline from their peak in North America, this will likely be the turning point Seabridge needs for the project and its shares to gain the momentum that market participants are patiently waiting for. But until then, that doesn't mean KSM isn't a great project - far from it.

We are not concerned about the inflationary risks of tariffs that, by creating long-term pressures, force North American central bankers to keep interest rates at these high levels for too long. Because this would create even more damage to an economy that is showing clear signs of slowing after more than 3 years of hawkish monetary policy, with consumer sentiment at its lowest since November 2022, consumers accounting for almost 70% of US GDP, and investment stalling, as Alberto Musalem also warned about investment. Investment is stagnant, as we wrote in our other article about another gold company:

“ […] because companies do not draw from the current economic situation any reason to believe that demand can recover and be very prosperous in the future.”

Therefore, interest rates will be cut to avoid worse consequences for the economy, as bank analysts raise their recession forecasts. Although somewhat vague, they also predict continued inflation, albeit with some market rhetoric, in our opinion, to maintain a scenario that is not entirely undesirable for them, as long as it feeds the idea in the markets to keep returns on their investments as high and as long as possible.

An economic downturn or recession would primarily cause investors to flee to the safe haven of gold, allowing prices to reach new highs. This scenario, where interest rates are lowered to recreate the conditions for a subsequent economic recovery, is what we see as a turning point for the KSM project that provides significant positive momentum for Seabridge shares, rather than a situation where it lacks bite.

Rudi Fronk, Chairman and CEO of Seabridge, also said:

It will also enable us to hold our priority status with BC Hydro for the low-cost green energy which makes KSM such a robust project economically.

Two factors are crucial to profit: the selling price of the metals, which is determined in commodity markets, and the total cost of producing and selling the metals in the markets.

According to the 2022 PFS, the KSM project's total All-In-Sustaining Costs (“AISC”) are $601 per ounce. As the image below shows, this already places the project among the most cost-efficient gold mining companies in the world, averaging close to $1,500 per ounce.

From the screenshot, we can see that KSM's AISC will decrease from US$ 601/oz to US$ 426/oz if the gold price increases from US$ 1,742/oz to US$ 2,900, the copper price increases from US$ 3.53/lb to US$ 4.25/lb, and the silver price increases from US$ 18.00/oz to US$ 32.00/oz.

The profitability metrics for the KSM Gold and Copper Project improve dramatically: after-tax cash flow increases by 119%, and after-tax net present value (NPV 5%, calculated at a 5% discount rate) increases by 146%. The stock consists of 100.22 million shares outstanding, representing a net present value of $193.57 per share. This indicates incredible growth potential, as Seabridge's share price needs to recover from its current NYSE price of $11.23 per share to reach its Net Present Value. Furthermore, the after-tax internal rate of return also increases by 67%, with the initial payback period being shortened by 35%.

Therefore, it is easy to imagine the good impact that a further increase in commodity prices, as predicted by analysts and presented at the beginning of this article (gold price forecast of $3,300/ounce and copper price forecast of $5.50/lb), would have on KSM's profitability, as AISC would be reduced even further. It’s like a mechanism that's cranking up, ready to kick in with maximum force as the scenario described above happens: lower interest rates along with rising gold prices.

Rudi Fronk, Chairman and CEO of Seabridge, also said:

We will also be evaluating some of the engineering alternatives our prospective joint-venture partners have proposed for advancing KSM."

KSM Projects has a 2022 Preliminary Economic Assessment (PEA) that examines the feasibility of converting the mine primarily into a copper production facility, specifically using a copper-rich block mining system at the Kerr and Iron Cap deposits. At this “supergiant KSM-Iron Cap-Snowfield porphyry copper-gold deposit”, according to ArcWest Exploration Inc. ( AWX:CA) the production will last for 39 years, compared to the 33 years estimated in the 2022 PFS. Average annual production will be 366 million pounds of copper (compared to 178 million pounds in the 2022 PFS), 368 thousand ounces of gold (compared to 1.027 million ounces in the 2022 PFS) and 1.8 million ounces of silver (compared to 3 million ounces in the 2022 PFS).

This scenario benefits from the rising copper price. Considering that AISC (net of by-product credits) decreases from US$ 1.44/lb to minus US$ 0.12 due to an increase in copper prices from US$ 3.53/lb to US$ 4.25/lb, this leads to improvements in profitability metrics: after-tax cash flow increases by 100%, and after-tax net present value (NPV 5%, calculated using a 5% discount rate) increases by 120%. The stock consists of 100.22 million outstanding shares, corresponding to a net present value of US$ 127.71 per share. This indicates enormous scope for growth, as Seabridge's stock price currently stands at US$ 11.23 per share. Furthermore, the after-tax internal rate of return increases by 75%, while the initial payback period is reduced by 44%.

The copper price is heading towards US$6/lb.

The Stock Price Is Low But Has Not Bottomed Out

At the time of writing, Seabridge Gold Inc., listed on the NYSE under the symbol SA, was trading at $11.23 per share, representing a market cap of $1.15 billion.

Shares are significantly lower than our previous analysts, supporting a moderate buy, as they are below the MA Ribbon, and they are below the midpoint of US$15.345/share of the 52-week range of US$10.14/share to US$20.55/share.

In our view, shares could be on track to hit even lower market valuations. The dispute between the US and its major international trading partners, the EU and China, is now in full swing, heading into the fiery phase of the tariff war and hampering economists' sense of security in responding. The Federal Reserve may be more likely to maintain its thesis about persistent inflation while also reiterating the need for higher interest rates to remain on hold for a little longer than previously thought.

With zero-yielding gold unimpressed by higher interest rates as investor demand tends to favor US Treasury bonds, and copper also affected by having to discount a bleak outlook for consumption and investment, the company's shares could be sent to a lower market price reflecting the commodity sentiment and as still-expensive borrowing costs offset KSM's turnaround.

Conclusion

Seabridge Gold Inc. shares are rated “Buy” with a conservative outlook that seeks to capitalize on lower market prices. This is an upgrade from the “Hold” rating in a previous article. Largely driven by unprecedented gold and copper price increases, the company’s flagship KSM project in British Columbia, in particular, shines with its tremendous value proposition and awaits the optimal combination of macroeconomic factors so that it can finally take off.

Inflation concerns are now weighing on the sentiment of human insecurity among bankers who continue to buy gold, suggesting that borrowing costs may remain “higher-for-longer” a bit more than previously expected. Lower share prices cannot be ruled out for Seabridge Gold stock, so we encourage investors to continue to monitor them as they arise. However, we do not believe that central bankers can keep interest rates increased for much longer, for example, for quarters, as the economy is likely to enter a recession, and central banks will need to start cutting rates again to restore conditions that support a recovery. In addition, recession fears continue to drive investor demand for gold as a safe haven, and this will be reflected in the sentiment of Seabridge Gold shares as the profitability outlook for the KSM project highlighted in the 2022 preliminary feasibility study intrigues amid rising gold prices. This is a scenario that could provide a significant boost to the stock, recovering very quickly from its current levels.

The company also holds a preliminary economic assessment study for the KSM mining project, with a primary focus on copper mining. This offers greater potential in the long term, as copper is seen as a key metal of the future as we pursue an increasingly environmentally sustainable economy through the development of electrification, renewable energy sources, electric vehicles, and AI-data centers.

data.title: Alibaba Is In A Prime Position To Capitalize On Significant AI Growth In China
data.description:
One of the greatest opportunities facing the global economy today involves AI. To put the enormity of this opportunity into perspective, we need only consider that, according to an analysis done by PricewaterhouseCoopers, AI has the opportunity to contribute up to $15.7 trillion to the global economy by the year 2030. It's believed that $6.6 trillion of this increase will come from higher productivity, while the other $9.1 trillion will come from consumption side effects. This on its own is fantastic, and one of the largest beneficiaries of this movement will be China.

According to the aforementioned analysis, AI could add 26.1% growth in GDP to China by the year 2030. That dwarfs the 14.5% increase expected for North America. This could cause a lot of volatility as evidenced by the expectation that up to 26% of jobs in China could be displaced in the 20 years ending in 2037. However, this will simply involve the movement of jobs from where they are now to something new. The net benefit for that country could be the addition of 90 million jobs.

While American companies can and do play in the Chinese market, it is likely that the biggest beneficiaries will be firms that are domestic to China. Perhaps no candidate is better to look at in this regard than Alibaba (NYSE:BABA). Back in January of this year, I reaffirmed my ‘buy’ rating on the stock because of how cheap it looked and because of its overall potential. Optimism centered around growth in its Cloud Intelligence Group and its ecommerce operations has since pushed shares up a whopping 60.7% at a time when the S&P 500 is down 5.2%. Even with this move higher, there does seem to be a lot of opportunity for the company moving forward. And a lot of that will come from the AI explosion.

A country in transition

As a country, China has plenty of positive and negative attributes to it. The same can be said of probably any country. One positive thing that I can say, however, is that unlike the US, China plans for the long haul. This long term planning, combined with its centralized economy, has allowed the country to achieve rather consistent and remarkable growth over the past few decades. Other than briefly during the COVID-19 pandemic, the country has not seen a recession or depression since it began publishing quarterly GDP data back in 1992. In all likelihood, growth has been consistently positive since long before that.

When it comes to AI, China has continued to be forward thinking. Back in 2017, long before AI became a major aspect of any economy, the country announced its New Generation Artificial Intelligence Development Plan. This was a three-phase development plan aimed at turning the nation into the center of global AI innovation. This initiative saw the country focus more on promoting AI research, developing training and education programs to boost the skills of its workers, focusing on addressing regulatory matters in order to promote AI growth, and more.

Its more recent development is the East Data, West Computing initiative. You see, as the image above illustrates, you can really divide China up into two major sections. The eastern portion of the country is population dense. Around 94% of its population is based there. The other chunk of China accounts for only 6% of the nation's population. This unbalanced situation creates both challenges and opportunities. You see, the eastern portion of the country suffers from relative resource scarcity. The dense population requires massive resources in order to remain relevant and to grow. However, the western portion of the nation helps to make up for this, accounting for a tremendous amount of the country's renewable energy and other resource potential. Examples here include coal, natural gas, aluminum, copper, and nickel.

Recognizing this imbalance, the country set out to capitalize on the West and its resources. This has involved building out national computing hubs and data centers. One example can be seen by looking at Qingyang, Gansu province. Since this initiative began, that province has seen over 2,100 digital-oriented companies set up some kind of shop there. And in 2024 alone, the city spent $2.66 billion on data center construction and equipment manufacturing. Other major investments have also been made in that area because of this, including those involving telecommunications so as to facilitate high speed Internet access. The area expects to continue investing in these kinds of opportunities this year. As an example, Qingyang is focused on achieving 100,000 petaflops of intelligent computing power by the end of this year. That would be up from the 51,000 that it grew to by the end of 2024. To put this in perspective, 100,000 petaflops is capable of processing roughly 50 trillion images per second or enabling real-time road condition analysis for approximately 50 million vehicles at a time.

This is not to say that there aren't certain risks involved. At the start of this article, I mentioned the job displacement that should occur. There is always uncertainty during these volatile times as to whether the expected job gains will be realized. Furthermore, some reports have suggested that China might have gotten ahead of itself when it comes to the investments it is making in this space.

On March 28th of this year, an investigative report provided by Caiwei Chen, who contributes to MIT Technology Review, made the claim that many of the companies that have invested into building data centers are ‘treading water’, with Chinese news outlets estimating that up to 80% of the newly built computing resources sitting unused. The risk of overbuilding is particularly problematic in light of the fact that the Chinese company that released DeepSeek indicated that their approach to AI is significantly more cost effective than other leading AI models. Lower costs mean reduced demand for processing capacity.

In the event that technological improvements do not result in meaningful improvements on a large scale, another problem could be power consumption. Global data center power is expected to grow from 60GW to 127GW between 2023 and 2028. That's growth of 16% in an industry known for very sluggish growth. Obviously, China’s East Data, West Computing initiative is aimed at addressing this kind of problem. But it will remain a problem all the same.

Joe Tsai, Alibaba’s Chairman, recently warned that there could be a bubble starting to form when it comes to AI data center construction. This seems to be a global phenomenon. For this year alone, the four largest technology companies in the US that are focusing heavily on AI, Facebook parent Meta Platforms (META), Google parent Alphabet (GOOG) (GOOGL), Microsoft (MSFT), and Amazon (AMZN) have announced that they will spend around $320 billion on AI technologies and data center construction this year. U.S. President Donald Trump previously announced a $500 billion AI initiative called Project Stargate, though it remains to be seen how much of this will ultimately materialize. This seems to include planned spending from Microsoft that is already included in the $320 billion commitment I mentioned. It will also include investments from OpenAI, SoftBank, NVIDIA (NVDA), Oracle (ORCL), and others. All of this is slated to occur over a span of four years.

Even though Alibaba seems to be worried about a potential bubble, the company has stepped up hiring in the AI space. And according to management, they intend to allocate around $52 billion over the next three years to building out data centers and investing in AI in general. The company continues to make progress in other ways as well. In 2023, the firm made available on an open-source basis its Qwen LLM (Large Language Model), which is a focused on family of models generative AI.

The company has also made other moves in this space. Just recently, management announced that it had partnered up with BMW (OTCPK:BAMXF) in order to accelerate the integration of its LLM technology into BMW’s Neue Klasse vehicles. BMW also intends to adopt AI cockpit technology from a firm backed by Alibaba in upcoming models that are specifically focused on the Chinese market. These vehicles should start to be produced in China next year. In September of last year, Alibaba partnered up with semiconductor giant NVIDIA for the purpose of using its LLM technology in the autonomous driving space. And in February of this year, the company announced that it was partnering with Apple to offer AI capabilities for iPhones in China. This should help Apple considerably in a market that it has faced challenges in for a while.

Financially, Alibaba has been doing fine in this space. Using constant currency figures that are translated into U.S. dollars, the firm’s Cloud Intelligence Group has seen revenue grow from $14.13 billion in 2022 to $14.73 billion in 2024. Over that window of time, segment EBITDA associated with these operations jumped from $519 million to $848 million. We have seen continued improvements for the 2025 fiscal year. For the first nine months of this window of time, revenue totaled $12.04 billion. That compares to the $11.07 billion the company reported one year earlier. During this time, segment EBITDA shot up from $642 million to $1.12 billion.

In addition to doing well fundamentally, the company remains deeply discounted compared to similar firms. In the chart above, for instance, you can see how I valued it on a forward basis using the price to operating cash flow multiple and the EV to EBITDA multiple. For both the operating cash flow and EBITDA for the company for the 2025 fiscal year, I merely assumed that both of these metrics would grow at the same rate, year over year, as what the company saw for the first nine months of 2025 compared to the same time of 2024 when it came to EBITDA growth. By comparison, it is still trading quite a bit below similar competitors such as Amazon and Alphabet. Because it is a Chinese company and there are significant geopolitical factors at play here, not to mention regulatory factors, I would argue that Alibaba deserves to trade at a discount, but not at a discount this large.

Takeaway

Based on the data provided, I must say that I remain optimistic about Alibaba. The surge in share price that we saw is great and I cannot blame any investors for cashing out now. However, I would argue that the upside potential for the business based on recent fundamental data and the opportunity that exists in the AI space justifies more bullishness moving forward. Because of this, I believe that reaffirming the company as a soft ‘buy’ candidate makes sense right now.

data.title: Opera: Our Buy Zone Is Here - Rich Returns Ahead
data.description:
OPRA Is Finally Near Our Buy Zone After The Much Needed Selloff

We previously covered Opera Limited (NASDAQ:OPRA) in October 2024, discussing its robust prospects attributed to the improved monetization in Western regions, the recently refreshed Opera One for the iOS browser, and the 14 consecutive quarters of Rule of 40 financial outperformance across revenue/ EBITDA growth.

Combined with the compelling valuations, the rich forward dividend yields, and the double digits upside potential, we had reiterated our Buy rating upon a moderate retracement to our recommended Buy zone at between $14s and $16s for an improved margin of safety.

OPRA 1Y Stock Price

Since then, OPRA has further rallied by +20.2% to chart new 52 week heights of $22.50, before rapidly selling off by mid February 2025, attributed to the bursting AI bubble, the mixed macroeconomic/ geopolitical environment, and the higher recessionary risks.

Even so, we believe that the meltdown has been overly done, given that the global advertising spending is still expected to grow by +10.7% YoY to $1.08T in 2025, with digital ad spending expected to comprise over 75% of overall "media ad spending for the first time."

This is a relevant information indeed, since advertising is increasingly becoming OPRA's top-line driver at $93.32M (+21.4% QoQ/ +37.5% YoY) while comprising 63.9% of its FQ4'24 revenues (+1.6 points QoQ/ +3.9 YoY) - a promising development, since advertising is a typical highly profitable end market.

This is on top of its expanding partnership with Alphabet (GOOG), amongst others, with it contributing to the growing search revenues of $52.32M (+13% QoQ/ +17% YoY).

It is apparent that OPRA's intensified marketing/ AI-related/ data center investments have paid off extremely well, with it already triggering the robust top-line growth, as discussed above.

The same has been observed in the growing users from western markets by +53% and ARPU growth by 3.9x over the past four years to $1.97 in FQ4'24 (+18.6% QoQ/ +36.8% YoY), with it underscoring the management's ability to increasingly monetize its offerings, significantly aided by the higher Opera GX gaming browser users at 33.9M (+6.2% QoQ/ +21.9% YoY).

These reasons are also why we are not overly concerned about OPRA's moderating adj EBITDA margins to 22.6% in FQ4'24 (-2.3 points QoQ/ -1.9 YoY/ +9 from FY2019 levels of 13.6%) and adj EPS to $0.28 (-45% QoQ/ -28.2% YoY).

This is especially given the richer balance sheet at a net cash position of $126.79M (+35% YoY), allowing the management to incrementally invest in its long-term growth opportunities, as the North America and the EU region increasingly comprise 19% of its user base (+3 points YoY).

The Consensus Forward Estimates

This is especially since OPRA has already offered a promising FY2025 revenue guidance of $572M at the midpoint (+19% YoY) with stable adj EBITDA margins of 23.6% (-0.3 points YoY), with it building upon the robust FY2024 growth at +21.1% YoY and 23.9% (+0.3 points YoY), respectively.

These reasons may also be why the consensus have raised their forward estimates, with the browser company expected to report an accelerated top/ bottom-line growth at a CAGR of +16.4%/ +19.7% through FY2027.

This is compared to the original estimates of +10.7%/ +16.5%, while accelerating from the historical 5Y top-line growth at a CAGR of +7.5%.

Thanks to the richer net cash flow from operating activities at $104.97M (+26.8% YoY), it is apparent that OPRA remains well capitalized to pursue accretive investments and shareholder returns as well, as observed in the higher FY2024 capex by +1,148% YoY and ongoing share retirement by -24.3% since FY2020.

OPRA Valuations

These reasons are also why OPRA continues to be highly compelling at FWD P/E non-GAAP valuations of 14.11x, compared to the 1Y mean of 15.47x and the 5Y mean of 23.75x.

The same may be observed in its cheap FWD PEG non-GAAP ratio of 0.78x, compared to the sector median of 1.56x and its diversified browser peers, including Microsoft Corporation (MSFT) at 2.37x and Alphabet Inc. (GOOG), (GOOGL) at 1.07x, underscoring why OPRA continues to offer a promising high growth investment thesis, made more attractive by the recent correction.

So, Is OPRA Stock A Buy, Sell, or Hold?

OPRA 5Y Stock Price

For now, OPRA has lost all of its Q4'24 and Q1'25 gains, with the stock breaching numerous support levels while trading below its 50/ 100/ 200 day moving averages.

Even so, we believe that the correction has been a boon indeed, with the stock already entering our Buy zones while nearing the updated fair value estimates of $14.80, based on the FY2024 adj EPS of $0.96 and the 1Y P/E mean of 15.47x.

It has also triggered a richer upside potential of +57.9% to our long-term price target of $25.50, based on the consensus raised FY2027 adj EPS estimates of $1.65.

If anything, building upon the share retirement discussed above, OPRA also pays out rich annualized dividends of $0.80 per share with forward dividend yields of 4.95% at the time of writing, compared to the 4Y averages of 3.73% and the sector median of 1.68%.

Thanks to the robust dual pronged return prospects through capital appreciation and dividend incomes, we are reiterating our Buy rating, with the caveat the investors observe the ongoing correction for a little longer.

Given the higher CBOE Volatility Index and the still elevated short interest ratio at 9.7%, it remains to be seen if OPRA's recent bounce from $15 ranges may last, with market volatility likely to persist for a little longer until sentiments surrounding the mixed macroeconomic/ geopolitical environment lift.

Patience may be more prudent in the mean time.

data.title: ECB's March Meeting Minutes Confirm Diverging Views On The Timing Of Further Cuts
data.description:
By Carsten Brzeski

The most important passage of the European Central Bank's latest minutes was the discussion on the monetary policy outlook. It is obvious that at the March meeting, the views on next steps had already started to diverge. The more dovish ECB members stressed the risk of inflation undershooting as a result of escalating trade tensions, but also a stronger euro. On the other hand, the more hawkish team focused on sticky services and domestic inflation as well as the fiscal policy U-turn in Germany, justifying a more cautious approach to further rate cuts. As a result, few members argued that they “could only support the proposal to reduce interest rates by a further 25 basis points if there was also a change in communication that avoided any indication of future cuts or of the future direction of travel."

Looking ahead, the crucial question will be whether last night’s tariff announcements will convince the more hawkish team to agree to further rate cuts. The dovish team definitely seems to be on board. In short, if US tariffs on European goods are indeed implemented on April 9, this darkens the near-term outlook for the eurozone economy. We see little upside risk to inflation, unless the EU reacts with harsh retaliatory measures - something we don’t expect. Therefore, we think that last night’s negative trade and confidence shock and little prospect of quick negotiations will bring enough hawks into the team to cut the policy rate again by 25bp at the ECB’s April meeting.

Indexes are unmanaged, do not reflect the deduction of fees or expenses, and are not available for direct investment. The index data provided is not representative of any Lord Abbett product.

Market Review

  • Markets exhibited broader positive performance over the third quarter. Key performance drivers included the first of several expected rate cuts in 2024, where the U.S. Federal Reserve (Fed) implemented a 50 basis point (bp) cut in policy rates. There were also increased expectations for further rate cuts in 2024, driven by a more dovish shift from the Fed and hopes for an economic soft landing. This shift was influenced by mixed economic data, including softer consumer price index (CPI) reports and fluctuating job market indicators, which sparked recession fears but were mostly balanced by strong consumer spending and retail sales. Looking ahead, the bull case relies on continued Fed easing, disinflation and a stable labor market. However, high valuations, potential labor market issues, and wavering consumer confidence pose risks to this optimistic outlook.[1]
  • U.S. high yield bonds performed well over the quarter amid this environment, gaining +5.32%.[2] Short duration high yield bonds also performed well, returning +3.94%[3], yet underperformed broader high yield bonds due to its more limited duration exposure. The asset class benefitted from a contraction in high yield spreads, which moved 18 bps tighter, and a drop of around 100 bps in yield. Within high yield, lower-quality credit led the way with CCCs up +10.40%, outperforming both Bs and BBs which returned +4.59% and +4.27%, respectively.[4] Cable and Satellite TV, Telecommunications and Broadcasting were the top performing high yield sectors. In contrast, Automotive, Food & Drug Retail and Railroad sectors lagged. High yield issuance also continued to be robust with approximately $74 billion in new supply during the third quarter, following $78 billion in the second quarter and $87 billion in the first. 5 Default volume also remained relatively benign. The par-weighted U.S. high yield bond default rate finished the quarter around 1.64% over the last twelve months, which is more than 100 bps lower than it was at the start of the year.[5]

Fund Review

  • The Fund returned +4.18%, reflecting performance at the net asset value (NAV) of Class I shares with all distributions reinvested for the quarter ended September 30, 2024. The Fund’s benchmark, the ICE BofA HY US Corp Cash Pay BB-B 1-5Yrs USD Index*, returned +3.94% during the same period.
  • Security selection within the Telecommunications sector contributed to relative performance. The sector was one of the stronger performing segments of the high yield index over the quarter, and the Fund was overweight several lower-rated issuers that benefited from a lower interest rate environment and increased corporate activity around merger & acquisitions. Credit selection within Healthcare sector also contributed, specifically from underweight positions to the Pharmaceutical and Health Services subsectors, as these positions underperformed the index. Security selection within the Transportation sector also contributed, reflected by overweights to positions in the Air Freight and Logistics subsector that outperformed. An allocation to CCC credit also contributed to relative performance over the quarter, with lower-rated credit tiers outperforming higher-quality bonds over the period.
  • The Fund’s overweight allocation to and security selection within the Energy sector was a detractor from relative performance. The sector underperformed amid slowing growth prospects and a meaningful decline in oil prices. The Fund specifically held overweight positions in select E&P issuers, which were impacted most by these headwinds. Selection within the Basic Industry sector also detracted, reflected by the Fund’s overweights to positions in the Metals and Mining and Chemicals subsectors that also underperformed amid softer commodity prices. Security selection within the Automotive sector detracted from relative performance as the Fund was underweight companies in the Automakers subsector that outperformed due to idiosyncratic reasons.

Fund Positioning

  • We continued to be overweight B-rated credits.

The Fund remained overweight allocation to Bs relative to the benchmark, while underweight BBs. We believe that exposure to lower-rated credit currently offers the best relative value as we remain constructive on credit in general given the increased prospects for a soft-landing scenario. However, we increased the Fund’s exposure to BBs towards the latter end of the quarter as a reflection of concerns around economic growth. The Fund also maintained a modest allocation to CCCs, as we look to be selective in sourcing these potential investments that present more idiosyncratic opportunities as opposed to adding broader risk exposure.

  • We are more balanced in the Fund’s sector exposure.

The Fund remained overweight the Basic Industry and Energy sectors, which continued to be the top overweight sectors by a meaningful margin. However, we reduced this overweight, specifically in Chemicals, Metals and Mining, and E&P subsectors, as these areas tend to be sensitive to lower commodity prices and slower economic growth. Instead, we added to more defensive sectors, such as Utilities and Healthcare. The Fund’s top sector underweights remained Media and Services, albeit at more modest levels relative to prior quarters.

  • We added to sectors that have historically benefited from a lower interest rate environment.

With the Fed expected to implement further rate cuts in the near term, we have shifted focus to adding exposure in sectors that should benefit from a lower interest rate environment. These include sectors like Financial Services and Real Estate, as well as select opportunities in Telecommunications where certain companies have a more positive outlook amid a relatively more favorable refinancing environment.

  • The Fund maintained modest exposure to off-benchmark sectors.

We generally believe that select exposures to non-high yield sectors can offer attractive risk-reward opportunities, potential portfolio diversification benefits, and avenues for liquidity. While the Fund has the flexibility to toggle allocations to off-benchmark asset classes, such as bank loans and convertible bonds, we continue to view high yield bonds as relatively attractive compared to other sectors.

Outlook

  • We remain constructive on high yield credit despite the likely outcome of moderating economic growth.

We look to be more dynamic going forward given the potential for market fluctuations in response to above trend growth slowing to a pace more akin to trend. The Fund currently holds a more balanced composition between cyclical and defensive sectors, as we have reduced areas like Energy and added to rate beneficiaries like Real Estate and Utilities. Notably, fundamentals for high yield issuers continue to be robust with leverage and interest rate coverage also remain at supportive levels, aiding the belief that defaults should be relatively benign as we move into 2025. Key risk factors to watch remain a resurgence of inflation that upends the narrative accommodative monetary policy, a pivot by issuers to more non-credit- friendly uses of debt finance, and a macro or policy driven spike of interest rate volatility that would leave investors flipping to defensive with less focus on credit.

data.title: lululemon athletica: President Trump's Reciprocal Tariffs Kill The Turnaround Story For Now
data.description:
Investment Thesis

The last time I wrote about Lululemon Athletica (NASDAQ: NASDAQ:LULU), back in December 2024, I discussed the company’s third quarter performance and analyzed the company’s progress made towards stabilizing the declines seen in the Americas as well as whether the company’s momentum in its international markets remained sustainable. I had a BUY rating on the company.

Since the article was published, the company’s stock has tanked nearly 30%, including a more than 15% drop following its Q4 report, significantly underperforming the S&P 500, which declined 7% during the same period.

In this article, I analyze the company’s Q4 earnings report, which was released last week, and investigate how the company plans to navigate what is expected to be a challenging macroenvironment, especially in the company’s biggest market, North America.

A Snapshot of LULU’s Q4 Performance

LULU had a strong finish to the year in my opinion. More specifically, Q4 revenues came in at $3.61 billion, up 12.68% y/y and beating analyst estimates by $30.3 million. Diluted EPS came in at $6.14, up 16.1% y/y and comfortably beating analyst estimates by $0.27.

Gross margins came in very strong at 60.4%, which translates to an expansion of 100 bps (1%). Operating margins also were impressive, jumping 40 bps (0.4%) to 28.9%.

Despite a strong quarter, the stock crashed 14.2% post the earnings release and has been on a free fall since then. This drop can be attributed to a softer-than-expected guidance, both for FY25 and for the first quarter of FY25, as the company also fell victim to the punishing tariff policies as well as the overly cautious US consumer.

More specifically, for FY25, the company now expects net revenues come in between $11.15 billion and $11.3 billion (5% to 7% y/y growth), which fell short of the consensus estimates of $11.31 billion. Diluted EPS is now expected to be between $14.95 and $15.15, which fell considerably short of the $15.40 that analysts were expecting. For the first quarter of FY25, the company expects net revenue to be in the range between $2.335 billion and $2.355 billion, which fell short of analysts' expectations of $2.40 billion. Diluted EPS is expected to be between $2.53 and $2.58 versus analysts’ estimates of $2.74. Investors have to keep in mind, however, that the company still has $1.3 billion allocated solely to undertake future share repurchases.

Reciprocal Tariffs Kill LULU's Turnaround Story For Now

LULU cannot catch a break in my opinion. The company began last year with a major misstep as it failed to capitalize on the strong demand seen in the women’s category due to a failure to adequately stock up those choices that were in demand. At the same time, it was facing increasing competition from the likes of Alo and Vuori, who were also eating into the company’s market share. These twin shocks led to a substantial decline in growth in the U.S. market, and it took the company quite a while to stabilize.

By the third quarter, the Americas had returned to growth once again, and the decline in sales had stopped in the U.S. It also had a stellar holiday season, which enabled it to register strong margins as it did not have to rely on any markdowns. The quarter gone by was no different, as both revenues and EPS saw strong growth, and the gross margins once again expanded, further demonstrating the ability of the company to manage its sales without relying on markdowns. Net revenues in both Americas and in its International markets were impressive, and while the comparable sales in the Americas were flat, international comparable sales, largely due to China, grew 22% y/y on a constant dollar basis.

The company’s product launches, which included the likes of Glow Up, Daydrift and BeCalm, have been a hit with consumers, further demonstrating that the company’s ability to innovate continues to be strong and that the misstep of last year was a one-off shock. Daydrift, which is the company’s latest “lifestyle trouser for women,” has seen strong sales to the extent that the company now expects it to be a new core franchise going forward. The company also announced a strong product pipeline for 2025.

Finally, the company’s international markets continue to maintain its growth momentum. China, in particular, has been particularly impressive, as evidenced by the 39% y/y growth in revenues and a 27% y/y growth in comparable sales. This is an improvement on the previous quarter, when revenue growth was at 36% and comparable sales were at 23%. While the growth in both these metrics have not reached the levels seen in the first quarter, it has been heading in the positive direction with each passing quarter. Investors should also note that the steady increase in growth in China is happening despite the unaided brand awareness being at mid-to-high-teens.

Finally, the company continues to rely on its unique marketing strategy of community-based brand activations as well as launching member-only events to boost its membership program, which now stands at 28 million, an approximately 40% increase since the start of the year. Q4 was no different, as evidenced by events such as Membership Madness, where members gained exclusive access to in-store events and free classes at studio partners, opening a Glow Up Studio in connection with the launch of Glow Up, and partnership with Rock ‘n’ Roll Half Marathon in Las Vegas. All of these unique events paid off, as evidenced by the strong top- and bottom-line growth.

Overall, the company has taken all the right steps in my opinion, to correct the missteps of the first quarter of last year, and ensure that the company was back on the right track, especially amidst rising competition. Unfortunately, for the company, just as it was steadying the ship, the macroeconomic environment has become a lot more challenging, especially in the U.S., courtesy of tariffs and a US consumer who is devoid of confidence and has become overly cautious.

Every economic data related to the U.S. consumer has been flashing red recently. For instance, the University of Michigan’s consumer sentiment index, in mid-March came in at 57.9 from 64.7 in February, which translates to a y/y decline of 27%. The figure was the lowest level since November 2022. Spending on luxury items fell 9.3% y/y in February, much worse than the 5.9% y/y decline seen in January, which demonstrates that even high-income consumers, who make up a significant portion of LULU’s customers are cutting down on their spending as well. Then, there was the Conference Board’s consumer confidence index, which fell to 92.9 in March, marking its fourth consecutive month of decline. At the same time, the forward-looking expectations component of the index, came in at 65.2, which is a 12-year low. Finally, while the CPI report came in better-than-expected in March, the Fed’s preferred inflation gauge, the PCE index came in hotter-than-expected, further stoking fears of inflation picking up. Finally, from a U.S. economy perspective, in the latest Chicago Fed National Activity Index (CFNAI), the employment related indicators contributed +0.02, which was down from +0.08. While the sales, orders, and inventories indicators rose marginally, they still came in at -0.01. It was a similar picture for personal consumption and housing, which while jumping from -0.14 to -0.01, still showed a decline.

All the aforementioned economic data suggests that the US consumer has become cautious and has a negative outlook on the future of the economy. At the same time, if one looks at the guidance provided by WMT, a bellwether of the US economy, it also shows how the US consumer has significantly cut down on discretionary spending, an issue that was flagged by LULU’s management as well. Furthermore, investors, economists, and the US consumer in general, still have no clue the full extent of the reciprocal tariffs and what impact it will have, both in the short-term and medium-term on the US economy. For now, LULU’s management has projected FY25 gross margins to decline 60 bps, of which, approximately 20 bps is attributed to tariffs. However, the 20-bps headwind doesn’t take into account any impact from the impending reciprocal tariffs, which suggests that there is a possibility that it could get worse than expected. According to the company 2023 Annual report, 42% of its products were manufactured in Vietnam, and 10% were manufactured in Indonesia. 40% of the company’s fabrics were sourced from Taiwan. All these nations are in the so-called ‘Dirty 15’ countries and they were hit with massive reciprocal tariffs. More specifically, the tariff rates for Vietnam was 46%, Taiwan was 32%, and Indonesia was slapped with 32%. Additionally, China, one of the biggest international markets for LULU, was slapped with an additional 34%.

Overall, LULU has done a wonderful job in my opinion to steady its ship after a bizarre misstep in the first quarter led to a sales decline. Since then, the company’s ability to innovate and the various marketing strategies to boost awareness, have all paid off, as evidenced by the stabilizing sales in the U.S. At the same time, the international markets, driven by China, continues to perform remarkably well, with growth in revenues and comparable sales increasing sequentially. The latest products have all been a hit and the company’s 2025 product pipeline has been built keeping in mind the demand from its consumers. From a long-term perspective, the “innovation and newness” in the company’s products should continue to drive growth. For the foreseeable future, however, it is going to be choppy waters for the company courtesy of a more challenging macroenvironment, which just got a whole lot worse with President Trump’s reciprocal tariffs. Unlike the first quarter, the company cannot be blamed for today’s challenges, but unfortunately, the external challenges are far too powerful.

Valuation

Source: Company’s Q4 Earnings Release, LSEG Data (formerly Refinitiv), and Author’s Calculations

As mentioned earlier, both the revenue and EPS guidance for FY25 fell well short of analysts’ expectations, which was the reason behind the stock plummeting in excess of 15% post the earnings release. More specifically, the company now expects FY25 EPS to come in between $14.95 and $15.15, versus analysts’ expectations of $15.40.

If one looks at LULU’s results historically, then the company has had a track record of beating both analysts’ expectations as well as the high-end of its own guidance on a consistent basis.

As evidenced by the table above, on average, the company has beaten the high-end of its guidance by 10.13%. While this is impressive, one has to take into account that the FY21 beat has heavily skewed the results. If, however, we look at the difference between actual EPS and analyst EPS, then it has somewhat stayed consistent over the years, and the average magnitude of the beat is 1.81%. If I assumed that LULU would beat analyst estimates by 1.81% in FY25, it would have resulted in a projected FY25 EPS of $15.68. This is an assumption that I would have made, had President Trump’s reciprocal tariffs not been harsher-than-expected.

Unfortunately for LULU, they did, and given that the management, in their guidance only considered the impact from the initial tariffs on Mexico and Canada into their guidance, I expect the impact on the company’s bottom line to be far worse. As such, conservatively, I have assumed the lower end of the company’s guidance for my calculations, which are $14.95.

A projected FY25 EPS of $14.95 would imply an EPS growth of 2.2% y/y, which is a reasonable estimate, especially since the reciprocal tariffs were slapped on countries where the company sources a significant portion of its materials from and given the challenging macroenvironment driven by tariffs and a cautious US consumer. The 5-year historical median P/E of the company has been 31.7x. The projected EPS growth is about 28% of the long-term average EPS growth of 7.9%. As such, I have assumed a forward P/E that is at a 28% discount to the 5-year historical median P/E, which would be 22.8x, significantly lower than my previous estimate of 28.2x.

At a forward P/E of 22.8 and a projected EPS of $14.95, my updated price target for LULU is $341, significantly lower than my previous PT of $451, but still represents an upside of about 35% from current levels, especially after accounting for the additional 11% drop at the time of writing. Investors should also note that the short-term momentum in the stock is broken, as evidenced by the D grade on the Seeking Alpha Momentum Scale.

Overall, despite the considerable upside from current levels, I am still maintaining my BUY rating on the stock, rather than upgrading it to STRONG BUY. Moreover, I would strongly recommend a dollar-cost averaging strategy. This is also because there are considerable headwinds for the stock in the near term, which would imply much higher volatility. I have a very small position in LULU, but at considerably higher price levels than where it is trading now. Despite this, I am still waiting for the noise surrounding the reciprocal tariffs to quieten down before adding more to my existing position.

Risk Factors

My risk factors remain the same as my last article. The situation in North America in general, and the U.S., continues to remain challenging. As mentioned earlier, the management, during the earnings call, reiterated that they “remain focused on what we can control.” My bull case predominantly rests on the premise that the “newness and innovation,” should eventually make up, in the second half of the year, for the slowdown in sales growth in the first half of the year, after consumers get adjusted to the new tariff-induced normal. Any further delays to pick up in consumer spending can be detrimental to both revenue and EPS growth, especially as the company continues to make investments in line with its Power of Three x 2 strategy.

In addition to increasing competition in the U.S., there is also the possibility that a full-blown global trade war could have a negative impact on sales of the company’s international markets, especially China, where the company has been registering impressive growth. The odds of this have considerably increased now that the Trump Administration have imposed harsher tariffs than expected. Should this permanently materialize, then it would be an additional shock that the company would have to deal with, which would once again dampen the company’s growth.

Concluding Thoughts

LULU ended the year on a high, with a strong beat on both the top- and bottom-line estimates. The company’s sales in the US stabilized whereas it’s international markets continued their positive momentum. After a bad start to the year due to the company’s own errors, management has steadied the ship. Unfortunately for investors, the ship is heading into choppy waters, in the form of a challenging macroenvironment driven by a cautious US consumer and increased odds of a full-blown trade war thanks to harsher-than-expected reciprocal tariffs imposed by the Trump Administration. Even the management’s guidance for FY25 did not account for the latter.

From a valuation perspective, the stock following the post-earnings selloffs as well as the post-reciprocal tariff selloff continues to be attractive at current levels. However, its momentum has completely broken down. As such, while I am maintaining my BUY rating on the stock, just like the sentiment of the US consumer today, caution is warranted for both existing and potential investors.

Indexes are unmanaged, do not reflect the deduction of fees or expenses, and are not available for direct investment. The index data provided is not representative of any Lord Abbett product.

Market Review

  • In the fourth quarter, U.S. Treasury yields rose significantly, and the yield curve steepened, resulting in the Bloomberg U.S. Aggregate Index ending the quarter down over 3.0%, while U.S. equities experienced positive returns, led by a strong rally in large cap growth stocks. Markets were driven by the November presidential election, which saw a surge in investor optimism due to expectations of stronger economic growth, improved profit forecasts, and a more favorable regulatory environment. There were several bearish factors, however, including uncertainties regarding the incoming administration's policy proposals, debt and deficit concerns, and strong dollar headwinds. Economic data was mixed, with a rebound in November nonfarm payrolls but stalled progress on inflation. The U.S. Federal Reserve (the Fed) cut rates by 25 basis points in November and December. Although, the Fed adopted a more hawkish stance, projecting fewer rate cuts for 2025 than previously anticipated.[1]
  • Credit sectors performed well over the quarter, as investment grade corporate[2] spreads tightened 10bps, commercial mortgage-backed securities (CMBS)[3] spreads tightened 24 bps, and asset-backed securities (ABS)[4] spreads tightened by 20 bps. The 2-Year U.S. Treasury yield increased from 3.64% to 4.24% and the 10-Year U.S. Treasury yield increased from 3.79% to 4.57%.[1]

Portfolio Review

  • The Fund returned -0.10%, reflecting performance at the net asset value (NAV) of Class I shares with all distributions reinvested, for the quarter ended December 31st, 2024. The Fund's benchmark, the Bloomberg U.S. TIPS 1-5 Year Index*, returned -0.49% during the same period.
  • The Fund maintains an allocation to CPI Swaps designed to protect investors' purchasing power during periods of rising inflation. As inflation expectations increased for most of the period, the Fund's inflation protection led to a positive impact on performance.
  • The portfolio's allocation to investment grade corporate bonds was one of the primary contributors to relative returns, particularly within the Financial, Energy and Consumer sectors. Within Financials, we continue to favor diversified financial institutions with strong management teams and business models less dependent on deposits. Over the quarter, we decreased the portfolio's allocation to investment grade corporates, monetizing positions mainly within the Financial and Utility sectors that have performed well.
  • The portfolio's allocation to short-term high yield corporate bonds also contributed to relative performance given the sector's greater carry over the benchmark. Over the quarter, we increased the portfolio's exposure to the sector, focusing on higher-rated securities with short average lives as well as positions we believe have the potential to be upgraded to investment grade.
  • An allocation to asset backed securities led to a positive impact on relative performance given a compression of ABS credit spreads and the sector's outperformance.
  • The portfolio's modest allocation to emerging market (EM) sovereign bonds slightly detracted from relative performance. Although these holdings represent a small portion of the portfolio, we view EM sovereigns as a high-quality, liquid means to enhance carry. We are focused on individual opportunities in the sector that are attractive from a bottom-up perspective.
  • The portfolio's longer duration positioning relative to the index detracted from relative performance as yields moved higher over the period.
  • There were no other significant detractors over the period.
